title: Asuka None of this is the real me
display_title: Asuka None of this is the real me
wikitext:
[]: Asuka: "None of this is the real me"             Quote Post by Born of Lilith » Fri Mar 11, 2011 8:43 pm In episode 22, when Asuka is confronted with several embarrassing memories of things she has done, she claims that they aren't really her, which raises the question of whether she's just telling herself that as a defense mechanism or if it's true to some degree. I think the latter but I'll give arguments for both interpretations. She was just telling herself that: There's a saying that goes "it's the choices that make us who we are," and in his essay Existentialism Is a Humanism Jean-Paul Sartre asserts that the sum of who we are is defined by what we've done in and with our lives, regardless of circumstances outside ourselves that might've restricted our potential for something more. It's true: In "The Sickness Unto Death, And..." Shinji finds his innermost self which observes the surface, factitious self coerced by the external world. I think that Asuka's surface self dictated her actions so predominantly as to sort of bury the inner self. In other words, she was so chained by bad faith that she forgot that she was wearing a mask and that there was something, someone under it. She becomes removed from that crutch and becomes ashamed of the mask once she is in tune with her real, inner self again. Then she realizes how superficial, pretentious and disingenuous she behaved, and how untrue to herself she was. Thoughts?

[]:             Quote Post by Azathoth » Fri Mar 11, 2011 9:01 pm I believe that Asuka's catchphrases in that scene are being read by Mitsuishi, Hayashibara, and possibly Yamaguchi as well? I interpreted the scene to mean: "You have defined your life by actions and catchphrases, but anybody can take actions and spout catchphrases; this doesn't mean you're special." Asuka defends herself by arguing that there is more to her, that she does feel emotions for other people.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Fri Mar 11, 2011 11:50 pm I'd say Asuka knows damn well she's 'wearing a mask', and actively does everything she can to keep it firmly in place because she's afraid the real her doesn't measure up. What Arael is showing her is that the badass take-no-shit elite pilot routine looks very different from the outside, that what she thought of as cool and awesome is actually just bitchy and pettily spiteful. It's an emperor's new clothes sort of situation, a persona critique along the lines of "hey nice car bro, compensating much?"

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Sat Mar 12, 2011 12:38 am NemZ wrote: I'd say Asuka knows damn well she's 'wearing a mask', and actively does everything she can to keep it firmly in place because she's afraid the real her doesn't measure up. What Arael is showing her is that the badass take-no-shit elite pilot routine looks very different from the outside, that what she thought of as cool and awesome is actually just bitchy and pettily spiteful. It's an emperor's new clothes sort of situation, a persona critique along the lines of "hey nice car bro, compensating much?"

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Sat Mar 12, 2011 5:56 am The "shattering glass" sound effects would indicate that it's her "mask" being removed, i.e., her cool, arrogant, ambitious personality being just a front/compensation. Othes aspects may or may not be her envy/hatred for the other female characters she's trying to differenciate herself from, and yet, that scene shows that she knows very well that everyone can talk big like that if they want to. And, I'm not sure about this last point, but actions in ep 12, 17 and the alternate route she took in Rebuild suggest that she has a potential for kindness - the way she's frantically rejecting what she's seing ("None of this is the real me!") could suggest that she's also a bit disgusted by the arrogance of her usual self and the way she has been sexually presentig herself to others (Kaji, Shinji...)

[]:             Quote Post by LeoXiao » Mon Mar 14, 2011 10:47 am Here's the way I always looked at it: Asuka's exterior is a mask, she's hiding the "true" Asuka, etc etc. However, it's not that simple because the mask is actually a part of her, and she knows it. Without the mask, she'd be a different, unknown person, "True Asuka" notwithstanding. What would this "true" version of her be anyway? Asuka doesn't know, and doesn't want to know, so she keeps the mask, which has been "fused to her face", so to speak. Yet when the end comes and she has to face herself like everyone else, she knows very well that she reduced herself to a mask over the years, but has no way to face the uncertainty that might be her "real" self, that it might not actually be any better than the mask. She says "this isn't me" because she doesn't ultimately want to be her mask, but is distraught because she doesn't actually know what/who she is, and what opening that can of worms might mean. So Asuka was in a position that sucked and she knew it, but she didn't want to move out of that spot because there would always be a (high?) chance that whatever she did might suck even more or just the same. Understandably, this is one of the core scenarios that Eva brings up in relation to RL.

[]:             Quote Post by thewayneiac » Mon Mar 14, 2011 7:46 pm Of course, ADV complete blew it in the dub by having Tiff read all the lines herself.

[]:             Quote Post by esselfortium » Mon Mar 14, 2011 8:11 pm thewayneiac wrote: Of course, ADV complete blew it in the dub by having Tiff read all the lines herself.

[]:             Quote Post by COACH » Mon Mar 14, 2011 8:21 pm I still have a hard time telling who some of the voices are.

[]:             Quote Post by gatotsu911 » Mon Mar 14, 2011 8:43 pm esselfortium wrote: In fairness, though, when I watched it in Japanese (which was my first time seeing the DC of ep22 at all, I should note) I didn't even notice that the voices were switched with each repetition. So, that hardly "blows it"; it was already rather repetitive to begin with, and the distinction was pretty unobvious and easy to miss.

[]:             Quote Post by C.A.P. » Mon Mar 14, 2011 9:00 pm gatotsu911 wrote: You'd think it would at least have been in the Japanese script, though. For them to completely miss something that significant is just kind of bizarre, and one of the bigger flubs in the dub overall.

[]:             Quote Post by gatotsu911 » Mon Mar 14, 2011 9:14 pm C.A.P. wrote: Agreed. To most fans, changing a slap to a gun sound effect was a bad idea, but to me, not getting the actors to say those lines and stay to that scene's intention was worse. Way worse.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Tue Mar 15, 2011 12:40 pm esselfortium wrote: Presumably because they didn't have all the needed actors available when they dubbed the Director's Cut scenes. In fairness, though, when I watched it in Japanese (which was my first time seeing the DC of ep22 at all, I should note) I didn't even notice that the voices were switched with each repetition. So, that hardly "blows it"; it was already rather repetitive to begin with, and the distinction was pretty unobvious and easy to miss.

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Tue Mar 15, 2011 1:17 pm For whatever it's worth: Renewal/Platinum DC22' Commentary wrote: In the same struggle in her innner space in the "video version," when the voice that speaks with Asuka's mothers voice asks, "Who are you?" various Asukas with different voices appear as if in response. Those playing her various voices are female cast members we are familiar with: Kotono Mitsuishi, Megumi Hayashibara, Miki Nagasawa, Yuriko Yamaguchi, and Junko Iwao.

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Tue Mar 15, 2011 1:47 pm esselfortium wrote: I watched it in Japanese (which was my first time seeing the DC of ep22 at all, I should note) I didn't even notice that the voices were switched with each repetition.

[]: Re: Asuka: "None of this is the real me"             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Tue Mar 15, 2011 3:29 pm Born of Lilith wrote: In episode 22, when Asuka is confronted with several embarrassing memories of things she has done, she claims that they aren't really her, which raises the question of whether she's just telling herself that as a defense mechanism or if it's true to some degree.

[]:             Quote Post by COACH » Tue Mar 15, 2011 4:29 pm Sailor Star Dust wrote: For whatever it's worth: In order, it's Misato, Rei, Maya, Ritsuko, and Hikari.

[]:             Quote Post by Warren Peace » Tue Mar 15, 2011 5:10 pm Sailor Star Dust wrote: For whatever it's worth: In order, it's Misato, Rei, Maya, Ritsuko, and Hikari.

[]:             Quote Post by gatotsu911 » Tue Mar 15, 2011 5:43 pm Warren Peace wrote: Wait, why Maya? Asuka probably doesn't care a thing for her. Megumi Ogata would have been a more interesting choice.

[]:             Quote Post by Azathoth » Tue Mar 15, 2011 6:15 pm Warren Peace wrote: Wait, why Maya? Asuka probably doesn't care a thing for her. Megumi Ogata would have been a more interesting choice.
